chief_complaint: I get dizzy when I stand up

answers.intake_q1: When did the dizziness start, and does it happen every time you stand up?
answers.intake_a1: started 6 months ago and occurs most times I stand up
answers.intake_q2: Do you notice any fainting, chest pain, shortness of breath, or palpitations when you get dizzy?
answers.intake_a2: no, but I do feel a little faint when I get up
answers.intake_q3: Any recent changes in medications, fluid intake, or episodes of vomiting or diarrhea?
answers.intake_q4: Do you have any history of heart disease, diabetes, or neurological conditions?
answers.intake_q5: Have you noticed any weakness, numbness, or difficulty speaking or walking when you feel dizzy?
answers.intake_a5: difficulty walking - I stumble a little bit
answers.intake_q6: Can you describe what you mean by stumbling—do you feel unsteady or do your legs actually give out?
answers.intake_q7: Any recent falls or injuries related to your dizziness and unsteadiness?
answers.intake_q8: Have you noticed any changes in your vision, hearing, or experienced headaches along with the dizziness?
answers.intake_q9: Can you describe the changes in your vision when you feel dizzy?
answers.intake_a9: my vision is spotty like tv static
answers.intake_q10: Have you had any recent infections, fevers, or other systemic symptoms along with these episodes?

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 14-year-old female who reports experiencing dizziness that occurs most times she stands up, starting about six months ago. She describes feeling a little faint upon standing but denies any fainting, chest pain, shortness of breath, or palpitations. She also reports unsteadiness when walking, describing it as stumbling rather than her legs giving out. There have been no recent falls or injuries. She notes changes in her vision during these episodes, describing it as "spotty like TV static." There are no associated headaches, hearing changes, recent infections, or systemic symptoms. There have been no recent medication changes, fluid intake changes, vomiting, or diarrhea. She has no history of heart disease, diabetes, or neurological conditions. Patient is not pregnant., and she has no known medication allergies. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no vital signs or physical exam available.

A: 
The working diagnosis is orthostatic dizziness likely related to orthostatic hypotension or a vestibular/balance disorder given the recurrent dizziness on standing, unsteadiness, and visual disturbances. The absence of syncope, chest pain, or palpitations makes cardiac causes less likely, but neurological causes remain a consideration given the visual changes and unsteadiness.

Differential: 
1. Orthostatic hypotension 
2. Vestibular dysfunction (e.g., benign paroxysmal positional vertigo or other balance disorder) 
3. Early neurological disorder (e.g., migraine variant, demyelinating disease) 
4. Visual disturbance-related dizziness (e.g., ocular pathology or migraine aura)

P: 
- First-line medication: Not indicated at this time without further evaluation; focus on non-pharmacologic measures such as ensuring adequate hydration and slow positional changes. 
- Alternatives: 
 - Consider vestibular suppressants if vestibular cause confirmed after evaluation (e.g., meclizine 25 mg orally 1-3 times daily for 1 week; avoid in pregnancy and with caution in hepatic impairment) 
 - Referral to neurology or ENT for further evaluation if symptoms persist or worsen 
- Medication safety considerations: No known medication allergies; not pregnant; no renal or liver disease reported; medication dosing should be adjusted if these conditions develop. 
- Return precautions: Advise urgent in-person evaluation if the patient experiences fainting, worsening unsteadiness, new weakness, numbness, difficulty speaking or walking, chest pain, palpitations, or shortness of breath. 
- Testing: If no improvement within 48–72 hours, worsening symptoms, or recurrent symptoms: seek in-person evaluation for appropriate testing (e.g., urinalysis/urine culture or other workup as indicated).

evidence.title: Dizziness | Vertigo | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet:
For You 
Children 
Patient Handouts 
Summary 
When you're dizzy, you may feel lightheaded, woozy, or disoriented. If you feel like you or the room are spinning, you have vertigo. These feelings may make you lose your balance .
Dizziness can have many different causes. A sudden drop in blood pressure or being dehydrated can make you dizzy. Many people feel lightheaded if they get up too quickly from sitting or lying down. Certain medicines and problems with your inner ear may cause dizziness. So can motion sickness . Sometimes dizziness can be a symptom of other disorders.
As people get older, they may have more health problems and take more medicines. This makes them more likely to have problems with dizziness and balance.
Dizziness usually gets better by itself or is easily treated. If you are dizzy often, you should see your health care provider to find the cause.
NIH: National Institu

evidence.title: Balance Tests: MedlinePlus Medical Test
evidence.snippet:
eling that you or everything around you is spinning 
Feeling as if you're going to fall 
Feeling lightheaded or as if you are going to faint 
Balance disorders can be mild or so severe that you may have trouble walking , climbing stairs, or doing other daily activities. They can happen at any age, but they are more common in older people. Balance disorders are one of the main reasons that older adults tend to have more falls than younger people.
Good balance depends on your brain receiving signals about your position and movement from your ears, eyes, and the muscles and touch sensors in your legs. These signals also help your eyes stay focused on objects when you change position. Problems with any of the signals that are part of your sense of balance can cause dizziness and other symptoms.
The part of your ears that controls your sense of balance is called the vestibular system or "the

evidence.title: Vertigo - NHS
evidence.snippet:
e how often it happens.
Do
lie still in a quiet, dark room to reduce the spinning feeling
move your head carefully and slowly during daily activities
sit down straight away when you feel dizzy
turn on the lights if you get up at night
use a walking stick if you're at risk of falling
sleep with your head slightly raised on 2 or more pillows
get out of bed slowly and sit on the edge of the bed for a while before standing up
try to relax – anxiety can make vertigo worse
Don’t
do not bend over to pick things up – squat to lower yourself instead
do not stretch your neck – for example, while reaching up to a high shelf
Non-urgent advice:

Conditions A to Z
Low blood pressure (hypotension) 
Low blood pressure is a reading of less than 90/60mmHg. It does not always cause symptoms, but you may need treatment if it does. 
Symptoms of low blood pressure 
Get your blood pressure checked if you keep getting symptoms like:
lightheadedness or dizziness feeling sick blurred vision generally feeling weak confusion fainting This might mean your blood pressure is too low.
If you get symptoms when you stand up or suddenly change position, you may have a type of low blood pressure called postural hypotension.
Non-urgent advice: See a GP if:
you keep getting symptoms of low blood pressure such as dizzines
